trans-unit.source: What is GIS?
trans-unit.target: What is GIS?

trans-unit.source: At its core, GIS is a computer-based system to organise data with a spatial component (_geodata_). There are three core functions of GIS:
trans-unit.target: At its core, GIS is a computer-based system to organise data with a spatial component (_geodata_). There are three core functions of GIS:

trans-unit.source: Connecting data to maps
trans-unit.target: Connecting data to maps

trans-unit.source: Visualisation, organisation and processing of spatial data
trans-unit.target: Visualisation, organisation and processing of spatial data

trans-unit.source: Analysing spatial
trans-unit.target: Analysing spatial

trans-unit.source: In humanitarian work, Geographic Information Systems (GIS) serve as powerful tools. They utilize maps to present complex information clearly and efficiently. For instance, during emergencies, GIS can visualize events on a map, depicting where they occur, the extent of impact, and identifying primary needs. It aids in consolidating data from various sources, enabling quick comprehension of situations and facilitating better decision-making.
trans-unit.target: In humanitarian work, Geographic Information Systems (GIS) serve as powerful tools. They utilize maps to present complex information clearly and efficiently. For instance, during emergencies, GIS can visualize events on a map, depicting where they occur, the extent of impact, and identifying primary needs. It aids in consolidating data from various sources, enabling quick comprehension of situations and facilitating better decision-making.

trans-unit.source: __A more formal definition:__
trans-unit.target: __A more formal definition:__

trans-unit.source: A Geographic Information System (GIS) is a digital tool that integrates data with maps. It enables the collection, management, analysis, and visualization of data by associating them with specific locations on the Earth's surface. By leveraging GIS, we gain deeper insights into data, revealing patterns and providing a better understanding of the geographic context. This leads to more insightful analysis, improved communication, and ultimately facilitates better evidence-based decision-making. GIS is deeply rooted in geography, the scientific field dedicated to studying Earth's lands, features, inhabitants, and phenomena. GIS software is capable of displaying various types of data simultaneously on a map, enhancing our ability to comprehend complex spatial relationships.
trans-unit.target: A Geographic Information System (GIS) is a digital tool that integrates data with maps. It enables the collection, management, analysis, and visualization of data by associating them with specific locations on the Earth's surface. By leveraging GIS, we gain deeper insights into data, revealing patterns and providing a better understanding of the geographic context. This leads to more insightful analysis, improved communication, and ultimately facilitates better evidence-based decision-making. GIS is deeply rooted in geography, the scientific field dedicated to studying Earth's lands, features, inhabitants, and phenomena. GIS software is capable of displaying various types of data simultaneously on a map, enhancing our ability to comprehend complex spatial relationships.

trans-unit.source: GIS components
trans-unit.target: GIS components

trans-unit.source: GIS is more than a software. It’s a system and it includes multiple elements:
trans-unit.target: GIS is more than a software. It’s a system and it includes multiple elements:

trans-unit.source: Examples of GIS used by humanitarian organisations
trans-unit.target: Examples of GIS used by humanitarian organisations

trans-unit.source: To get a better understanding of how GIS is used in the humanitarian sector, we have collected some examples from different organisations in the section below (click the different tabs to view the examples by organisation).
trans-unit.target: To get a better understanding of how GIS is used in the humanitarian sector, we have collected some examples from different organisations in the section below (click the different tabs to view the examples by organisation).

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} Lebanese Red Cross This video gives an overview of GIS use in the Lebanese Red Cross in their operations, including informing ambulance services and supporting blood donations.
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} Lebanese Red Cross This video gives an overview of GIS use in the Lebanese Red Cross in their operations, including informing ambulance services and supporting blood donations.

trans-unit.source: YouTube video player
trans-unit.target: YouTube video player

trans-unit.target: <iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/BNOgW9Koz7A?si=gpXNpFRmjfoPV1dX" [#$sd1_sf7_tu2] frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen> </iframe> :

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} IFRC
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} IFRC

trans-unit.source: The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) publishes a wide variety of maps to support active operations. You can find some of those on [ReliefWeb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?advanced-search=%28S1242%29_%28F12%29) or on the IFRC GO platform's [Emergency pages](https://go.ifrc.org/emergencies/all).
trans-unit.target: The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) publishes a wide variety of maps to support active operations. You can find some of those on [ReliefWeb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?advanced-search=%28S1242%29_%28F12%29) or on the IFRC GO platform's [Emergency pages](https://go.ifrc.org/emergencies/all).

trans-unit.source: <iframe src="https://go.ifrc.org/" width="750" height="500"></iframe> :
trans-unit.target: <iframe src="https://go.ifrc.org/" width="750" height="500"></iframe> :

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} ICRC
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} ICRC

trans-unit.source: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has a specialised GIS Support Unit that runs their [GIS Resource Center](https://gisupporticrc.github.io/GISResourceCenter/#portfolio) and ICRC GeoPortal. The ICRC resource centre portfolio gives an idea of the kinds of analysis the GIS unit produces, although much of it is not public.
trans-unit.target: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has a specialised GIS Support Unit that runs their [GIS Resource Center](https://gisupporticrc.github.io/GISResourceCenter/#portfolio) and ICRC GeoPortal. The ICRC resource centre portfolio gives an idea of the kinds of analysis the GIS unit produces, although much of it is not public.

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} REACH Initiative
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} REACH Initiative

trans-unit.source: REACH Initiative is a humanitarian data collection and analysis NGO that has a strong GIS specialism. The [REACH Resource Centre](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/) is where the organisation publishes content, including [standalone maps](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category[]=information_products&category[]=data_methods&type[]=281&order=latest&limit=10) and [reports](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category[]=information_products&category[]=data_methods&order=latest&limit=10) which also often include maps and spatial analysis.
trans-unit.target: REACH Initiative is a humanitarian data collection and analysis NGO that has a strong GIS specialism. The [REACH Resource Centre](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/) is where the organisation publishes content, including [standalone maps](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category[]=information_products&category[]=data_methods&type[]=281&order=latest&limit=10) and [reports](https://www.impact-initiatives.org/resource-centre/?category[]=information_products&category[]=data_methods&order=latest&limit=10) which also often include maps and spatial analysis.

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} Médecins Sans Frontières
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} Médecins Sans Frontières

trans-unit.source: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) has a [GIS Unit](https://geo.msf.org/home) that publishes geospatial products on the [GeoMSF platform](https://geo.msf.org/catalogue) to support MSF activities.
trans-unit.target: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) has a [GIS Unit](https://geo.msf.org/home) that publishes geospatial products on the [GeoMSF platform](https://geo.msf.org/catalogue) to support MSF activities.

trans-unit.source: MSF uses GIS in four main areas:
trans-unit.target: MSF uses GIS in four main areas:

trans-unit.source: Operations, emergency preparedness and response
trans-unit.target: Operations, emergency preparedness and response

trans-unit.source: Healthcare
trans-unit.target: Healthcare

trans-unit.source: Advocacy, communication and reporting
trans-unit.target: Advocacy, communication and reporting

trans-unit.source: Environmental health
trans-unit.target: Environmental health

trans-unit.source: This short video outlines the role of GIS in MSF's response to the 2015 Ebola outbreak in Sierra Leone.
trans-unit.target: This short video outlines the role of GIS in MSF's response to the 2015 Ebola outbreak in Sierra Leone.

trans-unit.source: YouTube video player
trans-unit.target: YouTube video player

target.bpt: <iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/XxW_e9o0lA8?si=xS36hYai0rgmQW6J" [#$sd1_sf9_tu2] frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen>

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} World Food Programme (WFP)
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} World Food Programme (WFP)

trans-unit.source: The World Food Programme (WFP) produces maps and publishes geodata both on their own data platform - [WFP Geonode](https://geonode.wfp.org/) and on the [Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)](https://data.humdata.org/organization/wfp?). You can find maps published by WFP on [RelifeWeb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?advanced-search=%28S1741%29_%28F12%29). WFP's [Geospatial Activities Catalogue](https://www.wfp.org/publications/wfp-geospatial-activities-catalogue)outline's to organisation's geospatial services, which includes developing dashboards like this one one [cash assistance during the COVID-19 pandemic in Jordan](https://www.arcgis.com/apps/webappviewer/index.html?id=93b4605fff13415bb8d2decd0e9158e0).
trans-unit.target: The World Food Programme (WFP) produces maps and publishes geodata both on their own data platform - [WFP Geonode](https://geonode.wfp.org/) and on the [Humanitarian Data Exchange (HDX)](https://data.humdata.org/organization/wfp?). You can find maps published by WFP on [RelifeWeb](https://reliefweb.int/updates?advanced-search=%28S1741%29_%28F12%29). WFP's [Geospatial Activities Catalogue](https://www.wfp.org/publications/wfp-geospatial-activities-catalogue)outline's to organisation's geospatial services, which includes developing dashboards like this one one [cash assistance during the COVID-19 pandemic in Jordan](https://www.arcgis.com/apps/webappviewer/index.html?id=93b4605fff13415bb8d2decd0e9158e0).

trans-unit.source: WFP also builds dashboards for advocacy, like HungerMap Live:
trans-unit.target: WFP also builds dashboards for advocacy, like HungerMap Live:

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} iMMAP
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} iMMAP

trans-unit.source: iMAAP is an information management NGO that provides support to the UN and international NGOs. Their [product portfolio](https://immap.org/products/) includes examples of maps used in situation overviews, interactive dashboards and sector-specific analysis.
trans-unit.target: iMAAP is an information management NGO that provides support to the UN and international NGOs. Their [product portfolio](https://immap.org/products/) includes examples of maps used in situation overviews, interactive dashboards and sector-specific analysis.

trans-unit.source: ::::{tab-item} MapAction
trans-unit.target: ::::{tab-item} MapAction

trans-unit.source: MapAction produces maps and geospatial data support decision-making in emergency response. Their [maps and data](https://maps.mapaction.org/) page shows recent products they have published, and their [product catalogue](https://guides.mapaction.org/) gives an overview of the types of services they provide.
trans-unit.target: MapAction produces maps and geospatial data support decision-making in emergency response. Their [maps and data](https://maps.mapaction.org/) page shows recent products they have published, and their [product catalogue](https://guides.mapaction.org/) gives an overview of the types of services they provide.

trans-unit.source: GIS vs cartography
trans-unit.target: GIS vs cartography

trans-unit.source: __Cartography__ is the art and science of creating maps, encompassing the study and practice of mapmaking techniques. A Geographic Information System (GIS) represents a __modern evolution__ of traditional cartography, integrating advanced technologies and methods for spatial data analysis and visualization.
trans-unit.target: __Cartography__ is the art and science of creating maps, encompassing the study and practice of mapmaking techniques. A Geographic Information System (GIS) represents a __modern evolution__ of traditional cartography, integrating advanced technologies and methods for spatial data analysis and visualization.

trans-unit.source: While both cartography and GIS involve the creation of maps, they differ significantly in their capabilities and approaches. Cartographic maps typically present a simplified representation of geographic features, constrained by the limitations of physical space and the medium of the map itself. In contrast, GIS allows for the integration of vast amounts of spatial data, enabling complex analysis, modeling, and visualization beyond the scope of traditional cartography.
trans-unit.target: While both cartography and GIS involve the creation of maps, they differ significantly in their capabilities and approaches. Cartographic maps typically present a simplified representation of geographic features, constrained by the limitations of physical space and the medium of the map itself. In contrast, GIS allows for the integration of vast amounts of spatial data, enabling complex analysis, modeling, and visualization beyond the scope of traditional cartography.

trans-unit.source: One key distinction is that GIS facilitates the incorporation of diverse datasets, with virtually unlimited capacity for __additional layers and information__. This flexibility empowers users to conduct __spatial analysis, perform statistical assessments__, and derive insights to support decision-making processes.
trans-unit.target: One key distinction is that GIS facilitates the incorporation of diverse datasets, with virtually unlimited capacity for __additional layers and information__. This flexibility empowers users to conduct __spatial analysis, perform statistical assessments__, and derive insights to support decision-making processes.

trans-unit.source: In essence, while cartography focuses primarily on map design and visualization, GIS extends beyond to encompass spatial data management, analysis, and interpretation. Therefore, GIS serves as a powerful tool for cartographers and professionals across various fields to create, analyze, and derive insights from geographic information
trans-unit.target: In essence, while cartography focuses primarily on map design and visualization, GIS extends beyond to encompass spatial data management, analysis, and interpretation. Therefore, GIS serves as a powerful tool for cartographers and professionals across various fields to create, analyze, and derive insights from geographic information

trans-unit.source: What is spatial analysis?
trans-unit.target: What is spatial analysis?

trans-unit.source: Many map products rely on spatial analysis. And indeed, the ability to use analysis tools allows us to get the most out of the data we have and to solve a wide variety of problems. The points below give you an simple definition of the concept.
trans-unit.target: Many map products rely on spatial analysis. And indeed, the ability to use analysis tools allows us to get the most out of the data we have and to solve a wide variety of problems. The points below give you an simple definition of the concept.

trans-unit.source: However, the example of John Snow's cholera map of 1854 is a popular example of how spatial analysis can help to solve problems.
trans-unit.target: However, the example of John Snow's cholera map of 1854 is a popular example of how spatial analysis can help to solve problems.

trans-unit.source: Spatial analysis __studies entities and events__ using their topological, geometric, or geographic properties.
trans-unit.target: Spatial analysis __studies entities and events__ using their topological, geometric, or geographic properties.

trans-unit.source: It includes a __variety of techniques__ to analyse geographic data.
trans-unit.target: It includes a __variety of techniques__ to analyse geographic data.

trans-unit.source: Data can be __added to a map as layers and they can interact with each other__.
trans-unit.target: Data can be __added to a map as layers and they can interact with each other__.

trans-unit.source: GIS enables you to work with these __layers__ to explore critically important questions and __find answers__ to those questions.
trans-unit.target: GIS enables you to work with these __layers__ to explore critically important questions and __find answers__ to those questions.

trans-unit.source: An example from the past: John Snow's cholera map
trans-unit.target: An example from the past: John Snow's cholera map

trans-unit.source: In 1854, an __outbreak of cholera__ occurred in the Soho area of London, England. The most common theory was that the disease was spread through the air. Dr. John Snow believed that the danger was __in the water__. He made a map to analyse the __number of deaths__ in every housing block in Soho. He added the __location of water pumps__ on the map. He found a __correlation__ between one specific water pump and the number of infections.
trans-unit.target: In 1854, an __outbreak of cholera__ occurred in the Soho area of London, England. The most common theory was that the disease was spread through the air. Dr. John Snow believed that the danger was __in the water__. He made a map to analyse the __number of deaths__ in every housing block in Soho. He added the __location of water pumps__ on the map. He found a __correlation__ between one specific water pump and the number of infections.

trans-unit.source: __Dr. Snow's map of the cholera outbreak of 1854__ ({numref}`John_snow_zoom_map2`), and the reports that it accompanied, __won over the predominant "miasma theory"__ that the disease __was spread through the air__. Residents were now warned to __boil their water__, and so ended the last cholera outbreak London has seen.
trans-unit.target: __Dr. Snow's map of the cholera outbreak of 1854__ ({numref}`John_snow_zoom_map2`), and the reports that it accompanied, __won over the predominant "miasma theory"__ that the disease __was spread through the air__. Residents were now warned to __boil their water__, and so ended the last cholera outbreak London has seen.

trans-unit.source: This interactive version of the cholera map shows it overlaid on a basemap of modern London.
trans-unit.target: This interactive version of the cholera map shows it overlaid on a basemap of modern London.

trans-unit.source: __Using GIS__, several measures of spatial central tendency have been applied to the dataset, revealing that the Spatial Mean (the geographic center of the distribution of deaths) of the outbreak lies __within 35 meters of the Broad Street Pump__, identified as the __source of contamination__ in the 1854 outbreak.
trans-unit.target: __Using GIS__, several measures of spatial central tendency have been applied to the dataset, revealing that the Spatial Mean (the geographic center of the distribution of deaths) of the outbreak lies __within 35 meters of the Broad Street Pump__, identified as the __source of contamination__ in the 1854 outbreak.

trans-unit.source: Common map types in humanitarian response
trans-unit.target: Common map types in humanitarian response

trans-unit.source: The humanitarian sector tends to use certain types of maps regularly. These areoutlined below.
trans-unit.target: The humanitarian sector tends to use certain types of maps regularly. These areoutlined below.

trans-unit.source: General reference maps
trans-unit.target: General reference maps

trans-unit.source: Show important __physical features__ of an area
trans-unit.target: Show important __physical features__ of an area

trans-unit.source: Include __natural and man-made features__
trans-unit.target: Include __natural and man-made features__

trans-unit.source: Usually meant to help for __navigation__ or discovery of locations
trans-unit.target: Usually meant to help for __navigation__ or discovery of locations

trans-unit.source: Usually fairly __simple__
trans-unit.target: Usually fairly __simple__

trans-unit.source: Can be __styled__ based on the intended audience
trans-unit.target: Can be __styled__ based on the intended audience

trans-unit.source: Infrastructure maps
trans-unit.target: Infrastructure maps

trans-unit.source: Infrastructure maps in the humanitarian context provide visual representations of critical infrastructure such as roads, bridges, hospitals, and utilities in a given area. These maps help humanitarian organizations assess the accessibility of affected areas, plan relief efforts, and coordinate resources effectively during emergencies or disasters.
trans-unit.target: Infrastructure maps in the humanitarian context provide visual representations of critical infrastructure such as roads, bridges, hospitals, and utilities in a given area. These maps help humanitarian organizations assess the accessibility of affected areas, plan relief efforts, and coordinate resources effectively during emergencies or disasters.

trans-unit.source: Display relevant features and __structures__ in a specific area
trans-unit.target: Display relevant features and __structures__ in a specific area

trans-unit.source: Help __planning__ and navigation
trans-unit.target: Help __planning__ and navigation

trans-unit.source: High level of __detail__
trans-unit.target: High level of __detail__

trans-unit.source: Produced after field __data collection__
trans-unit.target: Produced after field __data collection__

trans-unit.source: Thematic maps
trans-unit.target: Thematic maps

trans-unit.source: Thematic maps display specific themes or topics such as population density, disease outbreaks, or vulnerability levels within a geographic area. These maps help humanitarian organizations analyze and understand specific issues or trends, guiding decision-making and resource allocation for targeted interventions and relief efforts.
trans-unit.target: Thematic maps display specific themes or topics such as population density, disease outbreaks, or vulnerability levels within a geographic area. These maps help humanitarian organizations analyze and understand specific issues or trends, guiding decision-making and resource allocation for targeted interventions and relief efforts.

trans-unit.source: Focus on a __specific theme__ or subject
trans-unit.target: Focus on a __specific theme__ or subject

trans-unit.source: Features on the map __represent the subject__ being mapped
trans-unit.target: Features on the map __represent the subject__ being mapped

trans-unit.source: Use __colours and shapes__ to display quantitative and qualitative data
trans-unit.target: Use __colours and shapes__ to display quantitative and qualitative data

trans-unit.source: Rise __awareness__ about a specific subject
trans-unit.target: Rise __awareness__ about a specific subject

trans-unit.source: Analysis maps
trans-unit.target: Analysis maps

trans-unit.source: Analysis maps are used to examine and interpret data, revealing patterns, trends, and relationships within a geographic area. They facilitate in-depth understanding of complex situations, enabling decision-makers to derive insights and make informed decisions for effective humanitarian responses.
trans-unit.target: Analysis maps are used to examine and interpret data, revealing patterns, trends, and relationships within a geographic area. They facilitate in-depth understanding of complex situations, enabling decision-makers to derive insights and make informed decisions for effective humanitarian responses.

trans-unit.source: __Analyse data__ in respect to their geographic location
trans-unit.target: __Analyse data__ in respect to their geographic location

trans-unit.source: Create __new layers of information__ from the interaction between multiple features
trans-unit.target: Create __new layers of information__ from the interaction between multiple features

trans-unit.source: Use colours and shapes __to help users__ understand specific events
trans-unit.target: Use colours and shapes __to help users__ understand specific events

trans-unit.source: __Support__ decision makers
trans-unit.target: __Support__ decision makers

trans-unit.source: Generally display a greater __level of detail__
trans-unit.target: Generally display a greater __level of detail__

trans-unit.source: Situation/descriptive maps
trans-unit.target: Situation/descriptive maps

trans-unit.source: Situation or descriptive maps provide a snapshot of specific conditions or events in a particular geographic area. They visualize key information such as locations of resources, population distribution, infrastructure, and environmental factors. These maps offer a clear overview of the current situation, aiding in understanding and communication among stakeholders involved in humanitarian operations.
trans-unit.target: Situation or descriptive maps provide a snapshot of specific conditions or events in a particular geographic area. They visualize key information such as locations of resources, population distribution, infrastructure, and environmental factors. These maps offer a clear overview of the current situation, aiding in understanding and communication among stakeholders involved in humanitarian operations.

trans-unit.source: Used to __better visualize__ a specific ongoing and/or past situation
trans-unit.target: Used to __better visualize__ a specific ongoing and/or past situation

trans-unit.source: Maps can include __narrative__ and graphic elements
trans-unit.target: Maps can include __narrative__ and graphic elements

trans-unit.source: Can be used in reports and/or to __raise awareness__ on a specific event
trans-unit.target: Can be used in reports and/or to __raise awareness__ on a specific event

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: :::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: :::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: Take a moment to reflect on what you've learned in this module. The questions below are designed to help you check your understanding of key concepts and identify areas you may want to review further. You don't need to submit your answers — this is for your own learning.
trans-unit.target: Take a moment to reflect on what you've learned in this module. The questions below are designed to help you check your understanding of key concepts and identify areas you may want to review further. You don't need to submit your answers — this is for your own learning.

trans-unit.source: __What are the three core functions of GIS?__
trans-unit.target: __What are the three core functions of GIS?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer GIS (Geographic Information System) is a system for capturing, manipulating, analysing, and visualising spatial data. The three core functions are:
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer GIS (Geographic Information System) is a system for capturing, manipulating, analysing, and visualising spatial data. The three core functions are:

trans-unit.source: __Data Capture__: creating (or digitising) spatial data.
trans-unit.target: __Data Capture__: creating (or digitising) spatial data.

trans-unit.source: __Analysis__: performing analyses or processing spatial data to gain insights or knowledge.
trans-unit.target: __Analysis__: performing analyses or processing spatial data to gain insights or knowledge.

trans-unit.source: __Visualisation__: Visualising spatial data on maps, charts, or reports to communicate information :::
trans-unit.target: __Visualisation__: Visualising spatial data on maps, charts, or reports to communicate information :::

trans-unit.source: __What is spatial analysis and how did John Snow use spatial analysis in his cholera outbreak map from 1854?__
trans-unit.target: __What is spatial analysis and how did John Snow use spatial analysis in his cholera outbreak map from 1854?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer Spatial analysis is the process of examining geographic (spatial) data to detect patterns, relationships, trends, and spatial structures. It involves operations like overlay, buffering, proximity, clustering, interpolation, and assessing how phenomena vary across space.
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer Spatial analysis is the process of examining geographic (spatial) data to detect patterns, relationships, trends, and spatial structures. It involves operations like overlay, buffering, proximity, clustering, interpolation, and assessing how phenomena vary across space.

trans-unit.source: In 1854, Dr. John Snow mapped the locations of cholera deaths in London (Soho district) alongside the locations of water pumps. He noticed that the cholera cases clustered around one particular pump (on Broad Street). Because there was a spatial clustering around that pump, he hypothesized that the water from that pump was the source of the outbreak. He then convinced authorities to remove the pump handle, after which the outbreak declined. In effect, he used spatial distribution and clustering of disease incidents to infer a causal relationship. (This is a classic early example of applying spatial analysis in epidemiology.)His map showed that the deaths were not randomly distributed but spatially concentrated around a water source, which is the essence of spatial reasoning.
trans-unit.target: In 1854, Dr. John Snow mapped the locations of cholera deaths in London (Soho district) alongside the locations of water pumps. He noticed that the cholera cases clustered around one particular pump (on Broad Street). Because there was a spatial clustering around that pump, he hypothesized that the water from that pump was the source of the outbreak. He then convinced authorities to remove the pump handle, after which the outbreak declined. In effect, he used spatial distribution and clustering of disease incidents to infer a causal relationship. (This is a classic early example of applying spatial analysis in epidemiology.)His map showed that the deaths were not randomly distributed but spatially concentrated around a water source, which is the essence of spatial reasoning.

trans-unit.source: __Name the five common map types used in humanitarian work.__
trans-unit.target: __Name the five common map types used in humanitarian work.__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: General Reference Maps: Show general geographic features like roads, rivers, and administrative boundaries; used for navigation and orientation.
trans-unit.target: General Reference Maps: Show general geographic features like roads, rivers, and administrative boundaries; used for navigation and orientation.

trans-unit.source: Infrastructure Maps: Thematic maps focused on physical infrastructure (e.g., hospitals, water points, roads); used for planning and logistics.
trans-unit.target: Infrastructure Maps: Thematic maps focused on physical infrastructure (e.g., hospitals, water points, roads); used for planning and logistics.

trans-unit.source: Thematic Maps: Represent a specific theme or dataset (e.g., population, rainfall, conflict); uses color, shapes, or symbols to visualize non-spatial attributes.
trans-unit.target: Thematic Maps: Represent a specific theme or dataset (e.g., population, rainfall, conflict); uses color, shapes, or symbols to visualize non-spatial attributes.

trans-unit.source: Analysis Maps: Result from spatial analysis to guide decision-making (e.g., hazard risk zones, suitability models).
trans-unit.target: Analysis Maps: Result from spatial analysis to guide decision-making (e.g., hazard risk zones, suitability models).

trans-unit.source: Situation Maps: Provide an overview of a humanitarian or emergency situation; show affected areas, operational activities, or needs assessments. :::
trans-unit.target: Situation Maps: Provide an overview of a humanitarian or emergency situation; show affected areas, operational activities, or needs assessments. :::
